nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, swamps, and shallow margins of slow-moving streams and ponds in the southeastern United States; grows both submersed and emersed

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate preferred but tolerates plain gravel or sand
open midground or background planting area
best displayed in small groups of stems

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, or large cichlids

summary: Lemon Bacopa (Bacopa caroliniana) is an undemanding stem plant native to the southeastern United States, recognizable by its fleshy oval leaves that emit a distinct lemon scent when crushed. Under stronger light, the upper leaves tend to develop copper to reddish hues.
careNotes: Lemon Bacopa is a beginner-friendly stem plant well suited to low-tech and planted setups alike. It is distinct from Bacopa monnieri (commonly sold as Moneywort) — the leaves are larger, thicker, and notably lemon-scented when crushed. The older scientific name Bacopa amplexicaulis remains common in the aquarium trade and refers to the same species; trade names such as 'Giant Red Bacopa' also refer to this plant. Generally advised to plant in small groups of stems in the midground or background; under stronger light the upper leaves tend to develop a copper, bronze, or reddish hue, while lower light produces taller, leaner growth with a uniform green color. CO2 is not required but tends to improve growth rate and coloration. Substrate is flexible — plain gravel or sand is acceptable, though nutrient-rich aquasoil and regular liquid fertilization will give fuller, more vibrant growth. Iron and potassium supplementation help maintain colored tops. A tank of at least 40 L is generally suggested for a small group of stems. Current taxonomy places the genus Bacopa in the family Plantaginaceae, though some older aquarium guides still list it under Scrophulariaceae.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via stem cuttings: trim a healthy lateral shoot or top section 8-15 cm long and replant directly into the substrate, where it readily roots. Regular trimming and replanting maintains a dense, bushy stand and encourages branching from the base.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source. Tropica describes Bacopa caroliniana as an easy beginner plant with low light/CO2 demand, slow growth, height 20-30+ cm, propagated by side-shoot cuttings. Tropica search URL provided in the manifest redirected to product page item 043 (id 4464).

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature range (64-82°F = 18-28°C), moderate lighting, moderate growth rate, midground/background placement, propagation by cuttings, and beginner difficulty.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Plantaginaceae, Lamiales), native range (southeastern USA and Mexico), full tolerance ranges (temperature 4-29°C, pH 5-7.5, kH 2-18), lemon-scent characteristic, and bronze/pink coloration under high light with low nutrients.

additional.name: Aquarium Breeder
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (6-8), GH (2-15), height (20-40+ cm), reddish top coloration under high light, lemon/mint scent when crushed, and beginner-friendly care level.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Trade-name source for 'Bacopa Amplexicaulis / Giant Red Bacopa'. Lists family Plantaginaceae, southeast US origin, moderate light (tolerates low), CO2 not necessary, pH 6.0-7.5, height ~10-30 cm, slow growth, midground placement, propagation by cuttings, beginner difficulty. Merged into this entry because Bacopa amplexicaulis is a taxonomic synonym of Bacopa caroliniana.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirms Bacopa amplexicaulis is a scientific synonym of Bacopa caroliniana (alongside Obolaria caroliniana, Herpestes amplexicaulis, Herpestes caroliniana). Used for taxonomy (Plantaginaceae, Lamiales) and synonymy confirmation.
